content.problemStatement:
The median of an array BBB of length KKK is obtained as follows:


Sort BBB in non-decreasing order, i.e. B1≤B2≤…≤BKB_1 \le B_2 \le \ldots \le B_KB1​≤B2​≤…≤BK​.
After sorting, the median is the element at index ⌈K2⌉\left\lceil \frac{K}{2} \right\rceil⌈2K​⌉ of BBB.

In other words, after sorting, if KKK is odd, the median is the middle element, and if KKK is even the median is the left of the two middle elements.
For an array AAA of length NNN, we define f(A)f(A)f(A) to be the set of integers that appear as the median of at least one contiguous subarray of AAA **that has length greater than 111.**

That is,

f(A)={median(A[L,R])∣1≤L<R≤N}f(A) = \{\text{median}(A[L, R]) \mid 1 \le L \lt R \le N \}f(A)={median(A[L,R])∣1≤L<R≤N}where A[L,R]A[L, R]A[L,R] denotes the subarray [AL,AL+1,…,AR][A_L, A_{L+1}, \ldots, A_R][AL​,AL+1​,…,AR​].

You are given an integer NNN.

Count the number of distinct subsets of {1,2,…,N}\{1, 2, \ldots, N\}{1,2,…,N} that appear as f(P)f(P)f(P) for at least one permutation†^\dagger† PPP of the integers {1,2,…,N}\{1, 2, \ldots, N\}{1,2,…,N}.

The answer can be large, so report it modulo 998244353998244353998244353.

†^\dagger† A permutation of the integers {1,2,…,N}\{1, 2, \ldots, N\}{1,2,…,N} is an array that contains every integer from 111 to NNN exactly once each.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of a single line of input, containing one integer NNN.


Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line the number of subsets SSS of {1,2,…,N}\{1, 2, \ldots, N\}{1,2,…,N} for which there exists a length-NNN permutation PPP with f(P)=Sf(P) = Sf(P)=S, modulo 998244353998244353998244353.
constraints: The sum of NNN over all test cases won't exceed 500050005000.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: For N=2N = 2N=2, the permutations are [1,2][1, 2][1,2] and [2,1][2, 1][2,1].
For both, f(P)={1}f(P) = \{1\}f(P)={1}. So, only one set of medians is achievable.
Test case 222: For N=3N = 3N=3, the only set of medians possible is {1,2}\{1, 2\}{1,2}, no matter the chosen permutation.
Test case 333: For N=4N = 4N=4, the achievable sets of medians are {1,2,3}\{1, 2, 3\}{1,2,3} and {1,2}\{1, 2\}{1,2}, obtained for example from permutations [2,1,3,4][2, 1, 3, 4][2,1,3,4] and [3,2,1,4][3, 2, 1, 4][3,2,1,4], respectively.
